evidence: Ramp's entire culture is organized around velocity, from decision-making to team sizing to process design, and Geoff Charles identifies speed as the core competitive strategy.

text: PMs should find, pitch, and close the first 5-10 customers for their products themselves before handing off to sales.
evidence: Ramp requires PMs to sell their own products initially, forcing them to iterate on positioning and pitch until they get it right, and if they can't find 10 customers, the product hasn't found demand.

text: OKRs should not be used for performance management because failing to hit a cross-functional OKR doesn't mean everyone tied to that goal underperformed.
evidence: Ramp focuses OKRs on things they want to improve vs. maintain, and each team is assessed by different function-specific metrics like quota attainment or customer satisfaction.

text: Hiring people with high slope (learning ability) over high intercept (current domain expertise) is critical at fast-growing companies because scope will be much bigger in six months.
evidence: Ramp greatly discounts domain expertise in favor of intrinsic curiosity and learning ability, and hires many former and future founders.

text: A venture-scale startup must have a path to $100 million per year in revenue and $1 billion+ valuation within 10 years.
evidence: Multiple VCs including Sequoia's Stephanie Zhan and Index Ventures' Nina Achadjian confirm this threshold as the minimum for VC fund economics to work.

text: When a great team meets a lousy market, market wins, making market size more important than team quality.
evidence: Classic advice from Andy Rachleff (co-founder of Benchmark Capital) cited by Lenny as a foundational insight for evaluating venture-scale ideas.

text: Making something people want is necessary but not sufficient for a venture-scale business; you also need enough people who want it and the ability to make enough money from them.
evidence: Lenny lists 10 examples of beloved products (Trello, Basecamp, DuckDuckGo, etc.) that likely generate tens of millions but will never be venture-scale.

text: Miro's AMPED structure (Analytics, Marketing, Product, Engineering, Design) brings all required functions into product teams from the start, resulting in more effective product development.
evidence: Varun Parmar describes how AMPED ensures diverse perspectives and includes Product Marketing from the beginning to think about positioning and differentiation alongside building.

text: Miro's general rule of thumb is to devote 60% of resources to product innovation, 20% to running the business and maintenance, and 20% to reducing tech debt.
evidence: Varun Parmar shared this as Miro's default resource allocation, noting it varies based on product maturity with some teams needing more tech debt reduction or product innovation.

text: Having OKRs at every organizational level creates overlapping initiatives, excessive process time, and too many priorities, so reducing OKR layers and frequency improves clarity.
evidence: Miro removed extra OKR layers, moved to semi-annual frequency, and limited objectives to 3-4 with 2-3 KRs each after finding quarterly multi-level OKRs consumed too much time and created duplication.

text: Monthly design reviews where the team reviews everything shipped and identifies what is and isn't high quality is more effective for raising the quality bar than abstract principles.
evidence: Miro iterated from the Mona Lisa principle (ship things you'd be proud to put your name on) to concrete monthly reviews with real examples of high and low quality shipped work.

text: Always run a correlation analysis before running a linear regression, because if variables aren't correlated, the regression will produce inconclusive patterns.
evidence: Olga Berezovsky recommends running correlation first to confirm whether X and Y are related before expanding to prediction with regression analysis.

text: Neither correlation nor linear regression proves causation, but correlation confirms the relationship between variables while regression shows how much one variable affects another and can predict behavior.
evidence: Olga Berezovsky distinguishes between correlation (relationship and connection) and regression (prediction and estimation), using MyFitnessPal food logging and retention as examples.

text: Product analytics tools like Amplitude Compass and Mixpanel Signal can quickly identify which features correlate most strongly with user retention.
evidence: The post demonstrates using Amplitude and Mixpanel to find that food logging correlates strongly with retention at MyFitnessPal while other features do not.

text: The most effective presentation structure is to tell them what you'll tell them, then tell them, then tell them what you told them.
evidence: Lenny found opening statements in trials unexpectedly impactful because knowing key points upfront prevented jumping to conclusions, connecting to the Aristotelian triptych and Minto pyramid principle.

text: Don't go into important decision meetings without knowing how key stakeholders will feel; do the pre-work through 15-minute pre-meetings to get alignment in low-stakes one-on-one discussions.
evidence: Lawyers never ask questions they don't know the answer to by doing extensive pre-trial preparation, and the same principle applies to PM stakeholder meetings.

text: Influence fundamentally boils down to two things: how convincing your evidence is and how much people trust you and your sources.
evidence: Jury instructions focused solely on evidence and witness trustworthiness while excluding attorney arguments, speculation, and emotion, yet produced the correct verdict.

text: Learning to work alongside AI will quickly become table stakes for product managers, just as Grammarly became for writing and Copilot for engineering.
evidence: Lenny draws a parallel between AI tools becoming essential for PMs and how Grammarly, Copilot, Firefly, and Midjourney have become standard for their respective fields.

text: The best way to prepare for the AI future in product management is to dive in and get your hands dirty rather than waiting for the technology to mature.
evidence: Dan Shipper's advice emphasized in the post, with 12 concrete PM use cases including summarizing feedback, roadmap ideation, PRDs, market research, and technical questions.

text: Standout PLG products disproportionately attract users through organic search/SEO (40% of new users) and product virality (16% of new users).
evidence: Kyle Poyar cites data from OpenView showing that companies like Airtable, Miro, Snyk, Webflow, and Zapier get 56% of new users from these two channels.

text: Building free sidecar products specifically designed to attract users through SEO is one of the most effective product-led marketing strategies.
evidence: Examples include Snyk's Open Source Vulnerability Database, Similarweb's Top Websites Ranking, HubSpot's Website Grader, and Ahrefs's Free Backlink Checker.

text: For PLG products, the target marketing KPI should be product activation (activated signups), not website traffic or monthly signups.
evidence: Kyle Poyar argues activated signups are the best north star for PLG marketers because they measure users who reach the aha moment, not just top-of-funnel volume.

text: PLG companies need to keep customer acquisition cost below $1 per unique visitor because the average PLG product generates only $1-2 in first-year revenue per visitor.
evidence: OpenView benchmark data shows a typical PLG funnel converts 6% of visitors to free signups and 5% of those to paid, yielding $1-2 per visitor before churn and costs.

text: Duolingo uses a co-lead structure with PM and engineering leads jointly heading each team, which provides complementary skills and divides leadership responsibilities.
evidence: Cem Kansu describes how the co-lead model proved more effective than single-function leads, combining business acumen with technical expertise.

text: Duolingo runs over 200 A/B tests at any given time, testing every product change as an experiment before rolling it out.
evidence: Cem Kansu emphasizes that Duolingo is experimentation-driven and data-obsessed, with PMs becoming experts in designing good A/B tests and analyzing massive datasets.

text: For mature product teams, balancing resources as a 50/50 portfolio between big features and incremental improvements is optimal because incremental changes compound but you must avoid local maxima.
evidence: Duolingo treats resource allocation as an investment portfolio varying by team maturity: 50/50 for mature products, 90/10 for pre-PMF products like Duolingo Math.

text: Feature-based teams without clear success metrics can measure progress through a combination of internal dogfooding buzz, user research, public forum sentiment, and long-term holdout experiments.
evidence: Duolingo's social features team uses Duo employee usage enthusiasm, Reddit/Twitter reactions, and 3+ month holdout experiments since social features take time to show effects.

text: Letting users pause or snooze a subscription instead of canceling is an effective retention strategy.
evidence: Listed as a concrete retention improvement tactic in Lenny's curated list of growth ideas from practitioners.

text: Narrowing your team's focus by removing one goal or project is one of the most effective ways to improve team velocity.
evidence: Listed as the first velocity improvement tactic, alongside no-meeting days and assigning a single DRI for each outcome.

text: Investing in resurrecting churned users is a high-leverage growth tactic since growth equals new users plus resurrected users minus churned users.
evidence: Listed as a retention improvement strategy with the growth equation framing that highlights resurrection as an often-overlooked growth lever.

text: The single most important thing to do to prepare for potential layoffs is to document your accomplishments with metrics, screenshots, and stories while you still have access.
evidence: The most common piece of advice from recently laid-off professionals was saving accomplishment details, since you immediately lose access to supporting evidence after layoff.

text: Creating distance between your work identity and your personal identity is essential preparation for career disruptions like layoffs.
evidence: Multiple laid-off professionals emphasized not letting work define you, with some reporting that being laid off opened new opportunities they hadn't explored.

text: The majority of growth inflections at successful companies sprang from a product improvement rather than external events or growth tactics.
evidence: Research into two dozen successful products found product improvements (Figma Team Libraries, Snap Stories, Netflix subscription model, Tinder Android app) as the most common inflection source.

text: The most durable growth inflections come from doubling down on a company's primary growth engine like SEO or virality rather than from one-off events.
evidence: Pinterest 100-1000x'd SEO traffic, Airtable's templates accounted for a third of site traffic, and Dropbox's Samsung partnership drove 100M+ signups by leaning into their core channels.

text: Long-term sustainable growth requires all three pieces: ongoing product improvements, ongoing events that spread the word, and a well-oiled growth engine.
evidence: Lenny synthesizes the research finding that while single moments can ignite growth, durable businesses need continuous improvement across all three dimensions.

text: Figma's biggest growth inflection was the release of Team Libraries in 2017, which provided so much leverage to design teams that it changed the competitive conversation.
evidence: Badrul Farooqi, Figma's first PM, identified Team Libraries as the feature that made Figma an obvious winner against Sketch and others.

text: GPT-3 tends to hallucinate confident but incorrect answers, especially when it lacks the right training data, making context injection via embeddings essential for accurate chatbots.
evidence: Dan Shipper demonstrated that GPT-3 gave wrong answers about Lenny's newsletter data but answered correctly when relevant article chunks were injected into the prompt.

text: Every newsletter, book, blog, and podcast used as evergreen reference information can now be repackaged as a chatbot, creating a new content format and monetization opportunity for creators.
evidence: Dan Shipper built a functioning Lenny chatbot in a couple hours using GPT-3 and embeddings, arguing this will become a standard way to interact with creator content.

text: Planning should take up no more than 10% of the execution time period, so quarterly planning should take less than nine days.
evidence: Coda CPO Lane Shackleton states this as a general rule their teams follow, evolving from two-week sprints to Quarterly Plus planning as the company scaled.

text: OKRs are not a strategy and should not be treated as a replacement for a strategy document that describes broader challenges with a clear why and how.
evidence: Lane Shackleton emphasizes strategy planning happens independent of OKR planning, and warns it's easy to fall into the trap of using OKRs as a strategy substitute.

text: The job of PMs and designers is to turn ambiguity into clarity, first for themselves, then for their teams, and ultimately for customers.
evidence: Shishir Mehrotra, Coda CEO, instilled this principle that Lane Shackleton considers core to product development philosophy at Coda.

text: Product teams should distinguish between input metrics they can control and output metrics like activation that are lagging indicators, and goal themselves on input metrics.
evidence: Lane Shackleton notes activation is hard to move as an OKR because it's a lagging output metric, so teams should goal on metrics they control and ask whether inputs will move the output.

text: If you want to be great at building product, your preparation should look more like a professional athlete's than someone winging it.
evidence: Lane Shackleton advocates practicing hard conversations 20 times, iterating on pitches 10 times, and doing 4-5 editing passes on communications.

text: Product-led growth is essentially data-led growth because without usage data infrastructure, you cannot activate or convert free users.
evidence: Hila Qu identifies data infrastructure (product analytics, customer 360 database) as an absolute must-have for PLG, noting many B2B companies lack sufficient product analytics.

text: Companies commonly skip buying an experimentation platform and jump to building their own, which is a mistake because it requires not just engineering but also data science and statistical expertise.
evidence: Hila Qu recommends smaller teams use tools like Optimizely, VWO, or Eppo rather than reinventing the wheel with homegrown platforms.

text: Heads of growth most commonly report to product leaders (CPO), but sometimes roll up into sales/GTM organizations to better align PLG incentives with revenue targets.
evidence: Hila Qu describes two common org structures: growth under CPO for product-native alignment, or under CRO to minimize conflict between growth and sales teams on quotas.

text: For B2B PLG products, there are four distinct aha moments for different personas: user, team, buyer, and paid-customer, and most companies stop strategizing after the first.
evidence: Hila Qu describes GitLab's experience where the pivotal activation moment was team-based (2+ users and 2+ features in 14 days), not individual user behavior.

text: Product-led activation should be product-led, not product-only, using a multi-channel approach including lifecycle marketing, human assistance, documentation, and forums alongside product onboarding.
evidence: Hila Qu emphasizes that while product onboarding touches 100% of users and is most scalable, the ideal PLG activation is led by product, powered by data, and supported by automated and human channels.

text: The biggest challenge when layering PLG onto an existing sales-led company is resistance from sales teams worried about quota impact and marketing teams worried about PQLs replacing MQLs.
evidence: Hila Qu identifies five common pitfalls including internal resistance, noting sales teams fear small-potato customers while marketing worries about workflow disruption.

text: True virality is mostly a myth; what we call viral growth is almost always driven by one-to-many broadcasts from people with large audiences rather than many-to-many friend-to-friend spread.
evidence: Yahoo researchers found 95% of news on Twitter comes from original sources or one degree of separation, and Derek Thompson's Hit Makers shows popularity is driven by the size of the largest broadcast.

text: Even the most viral products like Dropbox, Facebook, Snapchat, and Instagram show roughly linear growth when you zoom out, with viral moments appearing as short-lived S-curves.
evidence: Growth charts of Dropbox, Facebook, Snapchat, Slack, and Instagram all show approximately linear trajectories despite reputation for viral growth.

text: To sustain growth, you need both mechanisms of virality (word of mouth, invites, referrals) and a strategy of ongoing one-to-many broadcasts (PR, influencers, TV) to keep reigniting spread.
evidence: Lenny argues virality and turbo boosts go together like peanut butter and jelly; higher k-factor amplifies each broadcast, but you get no juice without large-audience exposure.

text: Founders should optimize their investor network for eigenvector centrality (how many people your connections know) rather than degree centrality (how many people you know directly).
evidence: Sam Corcos of Levels explains that knowing the most connected person in diverse networks gives vastly more surface area than having many connections in one dense network.

text: It's almost always the founder's fault when they aren't getting value from their investors, because investors have little context and need targeted, time-bounded, specific asks.
evidence: Sam Corcos sent 2,626 requests to investors over 3.3 years with a 63% response rate and 44% conversion rate by making asks targeted, time-bounded, and specific.

text: Investor requests are not like precious gems with limited uses; people like being helpful, and the failure mode is making bad asks, not making too many asks.
evidence: Sam Corcos found that investors often don't respond because asks are bad, not because they've been asked too many times, and maintains an abundance mindset about requests.

text: Building a startup in stealth mode is always a mistake because it inhibits frequent market signal and greatly increases time to product-market fit.
evidence: Suril Kantaria estimates stealth mode delayed key learnings about Savvy's market fit by six months, and observed the same pattern in other stealth startups.

text: Pre-PMF, you want either overwhelming market pull or no sales at all; steady sales with no acceleration is a dangerous riptide that can trap you.
evidence: Savvy experienced steady but non-accelerating sales that felt like winning but prevented them from recognizing poor product-market fit, and early large customers churned when cracks showed.

text: VC funding creates pressure that can delay honest assessment of product-market fit because founders tell themselves that if investors backed them, they must be on the right path.
evidence: Savvy raised $2.5M based on a vision rather than metrics, which committed them to a path and delayed pivoting even as the pandemic changed the landscape.

text: Headcount is a cost center, not a sign of progress, and the only metric that merits congratulations is product traction and product-market fit.
evidence: Savvy accepted accolades for growing the team when there was no underlying traction, and headcount later constrained their ability to pivot.

text: Figma deprecated traditional OKRs in favor of headlines, which are claims teams want to make by end of a time period, evaluated by a mix of quantitative and qualitative signals.
evidence: Yuhki Yamashita replaced a spreadsheet of hundreds of task-like OKRs with headlines that captured philosophical alignment and allowed creative measurement approaches.

text: PMs are responsible for making crystal clear why the team is solving a particular problem and why it's important over anything else, because clarity on the why empowers everyone to make great decisions.
evidence: Yuhki Yamashita insists that when the why isn't clear, PMs should invest in internal storytelling rather than jumping to solutions.

text: Using alignment widgets for anonymous voting on decisions in product reviews is more powerful than allowing the loudest people in the room to dictate outcomes.
evidence: Yuhki Yamashita describes how FigJam alignment widgets let people share and vote on sentiments toward specific decisions, improving product review quality.

text: The three-step process for finding your activation metric is: brainstorm potential aha moments, run regression to find correlation with retention, then experiment to prove causation.
evidence: Six real-world examples from companies show this process in action, from initial hypothesis through correlation analysis to structured experimentation.

text: Figma's core activation metric is collaboration in the same file with someone else within 24 hours, which allowed them to focus on winning design teams rather than solo users.
evidence: Badrul Farooqi, Figma's first PM, shared that users who experienced real-time collaboration were much more likely to become paying customers.

text: Slack's early activation metric was a team with 2+ users having 50+ messages within the first seven days, focusing on team success rather than individual user actions.
evidence: Merci Grace, former Head of Growth at Slack, explained the metric captures real collaborative behavior since email between two people isn't broken the way it is for groups.

text: The average activation rate across 500+ products is 34% with a median of 25%, and for SaaS products specifically the average is 36% with a median of 30%.
evidence: Based on a global benchmarking survey of over 500 responses, the largest survey of its kind, co-conducted with Yuriy Timen.

text: The most common and impactful ways to increase activation are simplifying onboarding UI/UX and reducing onboarding friction, not adding more features.
evidence: Survey of 500+ products found the top tactics were simpler onboarding, reduced friction, follow-up emails, optimized copy, and smarter targeting, with dozens of specific examples.

text: The most common mistake when setting activation milestones is setting them too early (just completing signup) or too late (multiple purchases), when the milestone should be a leading indicator of habit formation.
evidence: The survey found many companies define activation as signup completion or multiple transactions, both of which defeat the purpose of activation as a predictive and actionable metric.

text: A good activation metric should be predictive of long-term retention at a rate at least 2x better for users who hit it versus those who don't.
evidence: The benchmark survey defines a good activation metric as both highly predictive (2x retention difference) and highly actionable (something growth teams can directly impact).

text: A product team's strategic hierarchy flows from Mission to Vision to Strategy to Goals to Roadmap to Task, with each level informing the next.
evidence: Provides definitions and examples from Tesla, TED, Stripe, and others showing how mission drives vision drives strategy drives goals drives roadmap drives tasks

text: You need goals before you can align around a roadmap because when prioritizing ideas you look at ROI, and the impact you're prioritizing against is based on how much a project will impact your goals.
evidence: Explains that goals measure progress of strategy, and impact in ROI calculations is measured against goals

text: A strategy is essentially a short description of your plan to win, with 3 to 5 concrete investments that, if you get right, will bring you closer to winning.
evidence: References Tesla's multi-step strategy, Uber's staged approach, and Substack's three-part strategy as examples

text: The Racecar Growth Framework identifies five components of startup growth: growth engine, kickstarts, turbo boosts, lubricants, and mid-stage accelerants.
evidence: Comprehensive framework developed with Dan Hockenmaier showing how each component maps to a stage of growth

text: There are only four common growth engines: SEO, paid ads, sales, and virality, and the growth engine is the most important component because it is the only one that can be self-sustaining.
evidence: Maps growth engines to loops where output (revenue, users, content) feeds back into more growth

text: When just starting out, focus energy on kickstarts and turbo boosts until your growth engine begins to drive the majority of growth, then invest in lubricants.
evidence: Provides a five-stage sequencing guide for when to focus on each component of the framework

text: The nine most common kickstart tactics for getting your first 1,000 users include sharing with friends, reaching out to targeted strangers, pitching where your audience hangs out, enlisting influencers, getting press, creating viral content, physical placement, hosting events, and being first on a platform.
evidence: Lists tactics with examples from Cameo, YouTube, Airbnb, DoorDash, Snapchat, Tinder, and others

text: The primary growth engine for nearly all of today's biggest B2B products is sales, though some have a product-led or self-service element.
evidence: References a comprehensive table of B2B products and their growth engines

text: The GACCS framework (Goals, Audience, Creative, Channels, Stakeholders) is a marketing brief that keeps teams from doing busywork and drives cross-team alignment before work begins.
evidence: Emily Kramer created and refined this at Asana, Carta, Ticketfly, and Astro; endorsed by marketing leaders who adopted it

text: Much marketing work misses the mark because of six common failures: no goal, no prioritization, created for no one, work that does not stand out, no distribution plan, and experts excluded from the process.
evidence: Emily Kramer's analysis from leading marketing teams at multiple companies

text: Distribution is just as important as, if not more important than, what you are creating in marketing; if no one will see it, all the time spent making it is wasted.
evidence: Emily Kramer advises that if you have no distribution plan, you should wait on creating the asset

text: Areas of Responsibility (AORs) where each responsibility has exactly one DRI eliminate wasted time figuring out who to collaborate with and create extreme clarity on ownership.
evidence: Learned at Asana, used successfully at Carta; template provided with marketing-specific responsibilities

text: The seven strategies for improving influence are: make their goals your goals, charge your trust battery, help them see what you see, show success, bring evidence, leverage authority, and be likable.
evidence: Drawn from PM experience at Airbnb, supported by examples from the Superhost program launch and review system redesign

text: Trust in the workplace works like a battery that you charge by doing things that build trust and tap into when you need people to trust you.
evidence: Concept popularized by Tobi Lutke of Shopify; charged by delivering on promises, listening, helping, and assuming good intentions

text: Before asking someone to do something for you, take 10 seconds to reflect on what they want, then frame your ask in a way that highlights how it helps them hit their goals.
evidence: Demonstrated through Airbnb Superhost badge example where reframing the ask around guest-side conversion won over the search team

text: The more you rely on authority to get things done, the more your own authority fades because you become just a middle person without your own voice or decision-making powers.
evidence: Presented as a warning when discussing leveraging authority from executives and influential people

text: If a product or feature checks three of five criteria it is probably time to shut it down: less than 5% user engagement, more than 10% team resources to maintain, degrades user experience, misaligned with strategy, and very few vocal users.
evidence: Checklist based on research and Airbnb experience sunsetting Guidebooks, Wish Lists, Neighborhoods, Match, Apple Watch app, and Stories

text: Teams almost always wait too long to kill products; in the author's experience at Airbnb, not once did anyone regret a product or feature being sunset.
evidence: Reflecting on multiple products sunset at Airbnb including Milestones, Guidebooks, Neighborhoods, and others

text: A feature with low measurable impact can still be worth keeping if it supports the company's long-term strategy, as was the case with Airbnb's Superhost program which initially showed little measurable impact but was aligned with strategy.
evidence: Contrasts Milestones (killed after no measurable impact and strategy misalignment) with Superhost (kept because of strategic alignment despite low initial impact)

text: The recommended event to use for defining 'active users' in retention measurement is the main user action rather than visits, sessions, or logins, because it gives the cleanest sample of truly active users.
evidence: Olga Berezovsky's 10+ year experience as analyst at Change.org and MyFitnessPal; visits and sessions overcount while main actions provide precise data

text: SaaS companies commonly make the mistake of reporting one blended retention metric mixing free and paid users, which is misleading because paid users use the product far more than free users.
evidence: Analyst experience showing that blending hides the true activity of free users and the opportunity to convert them

text: Retention is an output metric and should not be used as a baseline for A/B tests because user activity is only one component of retention, and changes in activity often do not lead to measurable changes in retention.
evidence: Olga's experience seeing product analytics teams wonder why increased user activity didn't change retention metrics in experiments

text: X-day (bounded) retention is more appropriate for SaaS products tied to specific time bounds like trial or subscription length, while unbounded retention is better for B2C consumer transactional or social businesses with chaotic engagement patterns.
evidence: Olga Berezovsky's rule of thumb based on comparing SaaS subscription models with consumer social products

text: At Change.org, monitoring and slicing Day 7 and Day 30 retention of petition starters helped grow their 'petition starters per day' KPI by 450%.
evidence: Direct experience of Olga Berezovsky on the Petition Starters squad at Change.org

text: Channel partnerships and geographic expansion are two mid-stage growth accelerants that are rarely useful in early stages but can significantly accelerate growth when used effectively at the right moment.
evidence: Examples from Kayak/AOL, Netflix/DVD manufacturers, Spotify/telcos, and geographic expansion stories from Instacart, DoorDash, and Grubhub

text: Geographic expansion is one of the biggest growth accelerants a startup can have but is something you can only really do once, and normally happens post-PMF but before the growth engine is at full steam.
evidence: GTM sequences from Instacart, Tinder, Facebook, Spotify, and DoorDash all show geo-expansion between kickstart and growth engine phases

text: Channel partnerships are game-changing when they work but normally go nowhere or take far too long to make a dent; founders should treat them like any high-risk/high-reward opportunity.
evidence: Kayak/AOL, Netflix/DVD manufacturers, and OpenTable/Citysearch examples show both massive wins and the rarity of success

text: The majority of startups grow primarily through just one growth engine, and a common pitfall is trying to invest in too many engines at once without nailing any.
evidence: Nearly 100% of Tinder's growth came through WOM, Calm's through paid growth, and Thumbtack's through SEO

text: For consumer startups there are only three feasible growth engines since relying on sales is rarely economical: SEO, paid ads, and virality.
evidence: Analysis of growth engines across dozens of consumer businesses; sales only works as a growth engine for marketplace/platform supply-side

text: For marketplace and platform startups, supply driving demand is an incredibly powerful growth engine where suppliers market your platform to their own customers, as seen at DoorDash, Etsy, and Eventbrite.
evidence: DoorDash restaurants printed placards and stickers, Etsy sellers did grassroots marketing, Eventbrite organizers brought built-in demand

text: You will likely grow primarily through virality if you have to share the product to use it, the product is only fun with friends, or the product is super-fun to share.
evidence: Examples include Dropbox/PayPal (share to use), Snapchat/Facebook (fun with friends), Tinder/Airbnb/TikTok (fun to share)

text: At Airbnb, word of mouth was the biggest growth driver early on, accounting for over 50% on the guest side and over 70% on the host side.
evidence: Direct quote from Gustaf Alstromer, Airbnb's first head of growth

text: The four clearest signs of product-market fit in decreasing confidence are: cohort retention curves flatten, explosive growth through word of mouth, users feel the product is a must-have, and strong unit economics.
evidence: Research across dozens of successful consumer products including Tinder, Dropbox, Instacart, and Superhuman

text: Good six-month user retention benchmarks for consumer products are approximately 25% for social, 30% for transactional, and 40% for subscription, with great being roughly 45%, 50%, and 70% respectively.
evidence: Based on Lenny's prior research published in 'What is good retention'

text: Product-market fit should be thought of as a spectrum of confidence that changes over time rather than a binary you-have-it or you-don't one-way door.
evidence: Presented as a reframing of PMF, supported by the observation that PMF signs vary in intensity

text: There are only seven reliable ways to kickstart consumer growth and acquire your first 1,000 users: friends and colleagues, targeted strangers, going where your audience hangs out, enlisting influencers, getting press, creating viral content, and getting physical placement.
evidence: Research across dozens of consumer startups including DoorDash, Tinder, Snapchat, and Netflix

text: There are four strategies for crafting a remarkable hook: identify what is unique about your product versus alternatives, listen to how your most obsessed users describe it, determine what job your product does for people, and find what about your product grabs attention.
evidence: Examples from DoorDash (selling revenue), Behance (exposure), Netflix (no late fees), Robinhood ($0 commission), Dropbox (throw away USB drive)

text: If your pitch is not resonating, one of three things is happening: your pitch is bad, you are pitching the wrong people, or your product is not something people want.
evidence: Netflix iterated for 18 months to find the right pitch; Pinterest founder pitched tech people first before finding female bloggers were the right audience

text: DoorDash's Tony Xu spent weeks pitching restaurants on food delivery technology with slow results until he reframed the pitch as 'we are selling revenue' which changed the company's trajectory.
evidence: Direct quote from Tony Xu describing the pivot from pitching technology to pitching revenue

text: When deciding who to target early on, the target customer definition must be almost comically narrow, aiming for three very specific characteristics of your ideal early adopter.
evidence: Andy Johns (Facebook, Twitter, Wealthfront), Andy Rachleff (Benchmark), and Paul Graham all emphasize super-narrow targeting; examples include Wealthfront targeting 25-35-year-old engineers at pre-IPO tech companies

text: Pinterest initially failed by targeting tech friends, but when Ben Silbermann found that 30-something female bloggers at Alt Summit were the right audience, the product reached over a million users within a year.
evidence: Ben Silbermann's direct account of trying Apple stores, emailing tech friends, before collaborating with Victoria Smith of SFGirlByBay

text: TikTok's early growth strategy treated the platform like a new country where initial wealth should be distributed to a small number of creators to make them role models that attract migration from established platforms.
evidence: Direct quote from Alex Zhu, co-founder of Musical.ly and ex-president of TikTok, using the America/Europe metaphor

text: There are three common approaches to coming up with a startup idea: market first (investigate a space then find a problem), experience ripe for improvement (find a broken consumer experience), and problem first (start with a problem you have experienced firsthand).
evidence: Todd Jackson's research into Vanta, Flexport, Rec Room, LaunchDarkly, Pinwheel, Snackpass, Good Dog, and Cocoon

text: Sitting on a couch brainstorming startup ideas in a bubble is counterproductive because all ideas seem flawed without market feedback; founders should instead get out into the market and have real conversations.
evidence: Christina Cacioppo of Vanta spent months brainstorming before realizing it was unproductive; only found Vanta by interviewing founder friends about their pain points

text: There are six effective ways to build trust in a new marketplace: reviews, verifying supply, leaning on social proof, creating a perception of quality, providing a safety net, and delivering magic.
evidence: Research across Lyft, Thumbtack, Rover, Snackpass, Peerspace, Shef, Good Dog, and Udemy

text: High-quality photos are instrumental for building trust with customers in marketplaces, and Airbnb's professional photography program along with host profile photos contributed to immediate trust for new visitors.
evidence: Joe Gebbia confirmed design-for-trust mantra at Airbnb; Shef co-founder reported high-quality food photos instrumental for conversion; academic study showed host photos significantly impact guest decision-making

text: Udemy purposely had instructors set prices very high at $199 then discounted to $10 because high prices signal quality and trust to students while the low actual price drives conversion.
evidence: Archie Abrams, early growth leader at Udemy, describing their pricing strategy

text: The magic moment for marketplace trust is when both sides experience something better than expected: for Thumbtack pros it was getting hired and paid for their first job, and for consumers it was finding a pro better than through word of mouth.
evidence: Sander Daniels, co-founder of Thumbtack, describing their focus on liquidity to accelerate trust-building

text: Two-thirds of companies have both an IC track and a manager track for product managers, with the split typically occurring at the Senior Product Manager level (L6).
evidence: Analysis of career ladders from 20+ companies including Facebook, Google, DoorDash, Airbnb, Instacart, Lyft, and Uber

text: The most common PM title sequence is APM to PM to Senior PM to Principal PM on the IC track, and GPM to Director to Senior Director to VP on the management track.
evidence: Data from 20+ companies aggregated into a public spreadsheet

text: The most common attributes evaluated in PM career ladders are leadership, impact, scope, execution, communication, vision, strategy, collaboration, planning, and technical skills in that order.
evidence: Analysis across 20+ company career ladders

text: The three patterns of winning B2C subscription businesses are an obsession with efficiency, alignment between product strategy and acquisition strategy, and a singular focus on building a magical sticky product through rapid iteration.
evidence: Research into Noom, Duolingo, Grammarly, Calm, Spotify, Future, and Flo

text: Duolingo built a massive user base through word of mouth and no paid marketing by offering all learning content for free, because limiting free experience heavily slows organic growth since only payers can tell friends.
evidence: Cem Kansu, VP of Product at Duolingo, explaining their free-first business model creating an organic growth engine

text: Noom targeted one-month payback on paid acquisition which allowed immediate reinvestment into their performance marketing engine, making them less reliant on raising money to grow.
evidence: Nikhil Jhunjhnuwala, VP of Growth at Noom, describing how they scaled from $3-4M to $60M+ revenue with a team under 10

text: Future deliberately fired perfectly happy paying members in early cohorts to keep costs low and iterate on retention, only expanding operations once three-month retention consistently hit their anomalously high bar.
evidence: Rishi Mandal, CEO of Future, describing their cohort-based iteration approach to achieving high retention

text: Companies underestimate the power of experimentation volume; Noom ran up to six tests per week per PM, with 90% failing, because high velocity of learning beats high effort per test.
evidence: Nikhil Jhunjhnuwala at Noom describing their experimentation culture of fast learning with large minimum detectable effects

text: There are three common approaches to coming up with a startup idea: market first, experience ripe for improvement, and problem first, and these methods correlate with specific company categories.
evidence: Todd Jackson's research into 8 companies: Vanta, Flexport, Cocoon, Good Dog, Snackpass, Rec Room, LaunchDarkly, and Pinwheel

text: Vanta's founder knew she was onto something when people she had not even spoken to started reaching out directly to ask about using the product, indicating genuine market pull.
evidence: Christina Cacioppo's experience validating Vanta by first building a spreadsheet MVP then seeing unprompted inbound interest

text: For early-stage B2B businesses, investors focus on how quickly they ramp to $1M ARR after going live rather than month-over-month growth percentages, with reaching $1M ARR within 12 months considered good and 9 months considered great.
evidence: Consensus from two dozen investors including partners at a16z, Sequoia, Founders Fund, Lightspeed, and others

text: Best-in-class SaaS companies follow a triple-triple-double-double-double ARR growth pattern after reaching $1M in revenue.
evidence: Multiple investors cited this pattern including Nina Achadjian at Index and Jonathan Golden at NEA: $1M to $3M to $9M to $18M to $36M

text: For early-stage consumer businesses, investors focus on intensity of engagement, virality, and retention rather than revenue, with greater than 50% DAU/MAU or greater than 50% D30 retention considered excellent.
evidence: Mike Vernal at Sequoia and other consumer investors describing their evaluation criteria

text: It is a huge mistake to focus on the growth number before getting retention because you can hit great early growth by brute force with small numbers, but it will not translate to continued growth.
evidence: Ellen Chisa of Boldstart warning against premature growth focus

text: PM interview preparation has three parts: research the product and company, practice with real people through mock interviews, and study frameworks and core PM skills.
evidence: Based on interviewing hundreds of PMs; practice section placed before study because too many people study without practicing

text: Preparing for PM interviews gives candidates a big leg up even with just a few hours of effort, despite interviewers believing they can see through preparation to a candidate's true PM nature.
evidence: Author's experience interviewing hundreds of PMs over the years

text: Professionals should define their personal range of tolerance using three lists: intolerables to completely avoid, self-imposed boundaries that promote health, and a plan to flourish with items that put you in your optimal zone.
evidence: Andy Johns developed this after heart issues at 35 while president of Wealthfront, borrowing from biological range of tolerance concept

text: Mind hours, the time your nervous system is preoccupied with work stress outside of office hours, are the most emotionally taxing part of career progression and most people underestimate how many they accumulate.
evidence: Andy Johns's personal experience showing that nights and weekends used for recovery early in career become consumed by mind hours at executive levels

text: Nearly 50% of tech professionals surveyed had thought about changing careers at least three times because it was not worth it, and another 50% had cried because of work at least twice.
evidence: LinkedIn surveys run by Andy Johns

text: A career is better modeled as a series of S-curves stacked on top of each other, with acceleration phases followed by necessary recovery periods, rather than a single continuous upward trajectory.
evidence: Andy Johns's career included three major acceleration phases each followed by burnout and 6+ months off for recovery

text: The four most important marketplace metrics are fill rate (percentage of intentful sessions converting), bookings growth (completed transactions), supply growth (new active supply), and GMV growth (dollars through the system).
evidence: Based on Airbnb experience and conversations with a dozen marketplace founders and investors including leaders at Whatnot, TaskRabbit, Peerspace, and Snackpass

text: Fill rate is the ultimate measure of marketplace health because it bakes in supply quality, availability, and booking conversion, measuring whether people can consistently find what they want.
evidence: Alex Taussig of Lightspeed evaluates every new marketplace startup primarily on this metric; TaskRabbit uses it as their north-star marketplace performance metric

text: The founding insight for most great marketplace businesses is principally a 'liquidity hack' such as Uber paying drivers to circle neighborhoods, Airbnb paying for professional photos, and Faire guaranteeing items would sell.
evidence: Alex Taussig of Lightspeed describing how the prime directive of a marketplace is to generate liquidity where none existed before

text: 80% of marketplaces start out supply-constrained and about half stay that way, making supply growth a major priority for most marketplace businesses.
evidence: Based on Lenny's prior marketplace research series

text: You need to talk to an average of 23 PM candidates to hire one great one, with only 13% of inbound applicants making it to the first screen.
evidence: Data from Ashby platform analyzing over 75,000 recent PM applications across high-growth tech companies

text: The six core IC PM skills to interview for are communication, collaboration, execution, strategy, impact, and product sense.
evidence: Distilled from the 10 core skills of product management; product sense is not on career ladders but is tested in interviews

text: The project portion of PM interviews is the most informative component because it is the only time you see a candidate tackle a chunky new problem, watch them in action, and discuss their approach.
evidence: Merci Grace of Slack found project performance closely related to years-long success at the company

text: Companies should treat closing a candidate almost exactly the same as closing an investor, because candidates are investing something even more valuable than capital: their time.
evidence: Siqi Chen, CEO of Runway, uses the same pitch and demo for Series A investors and for candidates

text: Product sense is not something you need to be born with; it is a learned skill that can be developed through deliberate practice in empathy and creativity.
evidence: Jules Walter defines product sense as consistently crafting products with intended impact, built on empathy and creativity, and outlines four concrete practices to develop it.

text: Observing users interacting with products two to four times per month, paying attention to micro details like facial expressions and hesitations, builds stronger product intuition than reading research reports.
evidence: Jules Walter's experience at Slack showed that firsthand user observation revealed insights about mobile churn that led to experiments significantly increasing retention.

text: Removing many small annoyances from a product adds up to something meaningfully valuable, even when no single fix moves metrics significantly.
evidence: At Slack, Stewart Butterfield's obsession with details led to positive customer tickets thanking the team for thoughtfulness, even though users couldn't articulate exactly why they loved Slack.

text: Spending time understanding and reframing user problems with strong constraints is more valuable than jumping to solutions, because good framing naturally narrows the solution space.
evidence: Stewart Butterfield taught that proper problem framing sets opinionated constraints so only a few solutions remain, streamlining decision-making.

text: Keeping track of emerging technology trends at both macro and micro levels helps PMs develop creative product solutions by understanding what is newly possible.
evidence: Cash App's decision to support Bitcoin drove 76% of its 2020 revenue; Uber became possible due to smartphone GPS and Google Maps APIs; Figma leveraged performant WebGL.

text: Making a product free is an acquisition strategy, not a monetization strategy; it lowers CAC, increases virality, and gets attention in crowded markets.
evidence: Analysis of ~50 SaaS pricing models shows nearly every SaaS product offers some form of free offering to drive growth.

text: Nearly 90% of freemium SaaS products also offer a free trial of their paid tier, meaning most companies do not need to choose between freemium and trial.
evidence: Survey of ~50 SaaS companies found about 50% do both freemium and trial, and nearly all freemium products also offer a trial.

text: Companies should go with a trial instead of freemium only if their product requires significant hand-holding, has complex integration needs, or has a high price point.
evidence: Products like Okta, ServiceNow, and Snowflake use trials because they require complex integration or many stakeholders.

text: Freemium should not be implemented until a company truly understands how to convert leads to customers, typically 2-3 years into the business.
evidence: Patrick Campbell of ProfitWell advises that premature freemium increases noise and false positives when trying to find segment beachheads.

text: Waitlist-to-free-signup conversion averages around 50% if users are admitted within a month, but drops below 20% if they wait over three months.
evidence: Data collected from multiple founders across Twitter, email, and in-person conversations about their waitlist experiences.

text: Time on the waitlist is by far the biggest factor in conversion rate, as people quickly lose interest and forget what they signed up for.
evidence: Linear saw 30-50% conversion in the first few months dropping to 10% at 12 months; multiple founders confirmed this pattern.

text: Running waitlist emails through a bounce filter before sending invites prevents spam folder issues that can significantly reduce conversion rates.
evidence: Henrique Cruz at Rows found that releasing 5K invites at once caused hundreds of bounces leading to spam issues.

text: Good monthly churn for B2C SaaS is 3-5%, for B2B SMB/Mid-Market is 2.5-5%, and for B2B Enterprise is 1-2%, with great being roughly half those figures.
evidence: Consensus from 16 growth experts plus data from ProfitWell's dataset of 13,000 anonymized SaaS companies.

text: The higher the price point, the lower you should expect target churn, because smaller businesses are more likely to go out of business and high-CAC companies cannot survive high churn.
evidence: ProfitWell data shows acceptable churn at $5K+ monthly price points is below 0.35%; experts confirm the correlation.

text: Churn should be decomposed into intentional vs involuntary and soft vs hard churn to identify actionable improvement areas like failed credit cards and forgotten passwords.
evidence: Crystal Widjaja (CPO at Kumu, ex-Gojek) explains that overlooking involuntary churn causes companies to lose meaningful percentage points.

text: People do not get promoted for doing their jobs well; they get promoted by demonstrating their potential to do more through taking on increased scope.
evidence: Lenny's own experience: proactively mentoring junior PMs led to management promotion; leading cross-departmental projects led to senior PM promotion.

text: The most underappreciated and often most powerful ingredient in getting promoted is having an influential champion who fights for you in the calibration process.
evidence: Lenny's fastest career ramp-up occurred after moving to an influential manager who fought for him cycle after cycle.

text: If you have been stuck at the same level for over two years despite actively working on promotion strategies, it is probably time to explore a new job.
evidence: Lenny's observation across his 16-year career with approximately nine promotions across engineering and product management roles.

text: Angel investors are usually wrong about which investments will do best; only a third of the biggest winners were rated as high-conviction at the time of investing.
evidence: Lenny's review of 140+ investments found that if he only invested in high-conviction deals, he would have missed two-thirds of his best returns.

text: Angel investing is more about access to deals than picking ability; getting into hot rounds matters more than trying to find hidden gems.
evidence: Over two-thirds of Lenny's biggest winners were hot deals at the time; AngelList data shows broad indexing into credible deals outperforms selective picking.

text: Power laws are extremely real in angel investing: 70% of paper gains can come from a single company, and 80% from just four companies out of 140+.
evidence: Lenny's direct portfolio data showing extreme concentration of returns across 140+ angel investments.

text: The best way to build deal flow as an angel investor is to share great deals with other investors, as great deals function as currency in the investor community.
evidence: Most of Lenny's investments shifted from friends' companies to deals shared by other angels and solo capitalists over time.

text: Facebook Marketplace solved the classic two-sided marketplace cold start problem not by recruiting new supply, but by cross-posting existing items from commerce Groups into the new Marketplace tab.
evidence: Deb Liu describes the one-checkbox cross-posting experience that seeded supply by leveraging items already listed in Facebook Groups.

text: Facebook Marketplace users initially never used the search bar because years of Facebook usage trained them to scroll, requiring the team to add a prominent search button to change behavior.
evidence: Research showed users instinctively scrolled on Facebook while searching on competitor apps; adding a visible search button permanently increased search participation.

text: Trust through real identity was Facebook Marketplace's key differentiator over anonymous platforms like Craigslist, especially for high-consideration categories like cars and rentals.
evidence: Deb Liu's husband reviewed buyer profiles before meeting for a minivan sale; Facebook identity and tenure on platform provided comfort for in-person transactions.

text: There are only two paths to winning a market according to Michael Porter: performing activities better than rivals (operational effectiveness) or performing different activities (differentiation), and differentiation is more sustainable long-term.
evidence: Porter's research shows competitors copy operational improvements and erode margins, making differentiation the more durable strategy.

text: There are seven common ways to differentiate a product: be cheapest, highest quality, most convenient, safest, proprietary, feel-good to buy, or focused on a niche underserved market.
evidence: Analysis of hundreds of companies including Apple, Robinhood, DuckDuckGo, Patagonia, and DoorDash mapped to these seven categories.

text: Combining multiple differentiators creates an even stronger competitive position than relying on a single one.
evidence: Patagonia combines highest quality plus feel-good; Apple combines highest quality plus safest; Airbnb combined cheapest plus proprietary supply.

text: The single goal of an early-stage pre-PMF startup should be to make 10 customers very happy, and everything prioritized should serve this goal.
evidence: Founders from Ashby, Vanta, Stytch, Writer, Zip, and Mage confirm this approach; if you cannot make 10 customers very happy, you likely do not have a business.

text: Early-stage startups should not try to be data-driven because they simply do not have enough data for statistical significance; a 10% improvement at 20% conversion requires over 5,000 users.
evidence: Sample size calculator shows the data requirements are far beyond what most early-stage startups have.

text: Startups should not build everything users ask for but instead focus on understanding user pain points and independently designing the best solutions.
evidence: Julianna Lamb at Stytch explains that building what customers ask for without understanding the root problem leads to one-off solutions instead of universally applicable products.

text: For B2C businesses a payback period under 1 month is great and under 6 months is good; for B2B SMB under 6 months is great and under 12 months is good; for B2B Enterprise under 12 months is great and under 18 months is good.
evidence: Consensus from 16 growth experts including Andrew Chen, Casey Winters, Elena Verna, and Dan Hockenmaier.

text: Startups should focus on payback periods over LTV/CAC ratios because accurately calculating LTV for an early-stage company is highly suspect due to limited data.
evidence: Casey Winters explains that early-stage startups have very little data to predict lifetime value, making long payback periods especially risky.

text: One of the highest-ROI optimization hacks for mid-stage companies is running in-app promos and emails to convert monthly subscribers to annual plans, effectively getting customer loans to fund growth.
evidence: ChenLi Wang explains that annual plans create positive cash cycles, with some companies pricing annual at 6x monthly just to fuel growth.

text: The atomic network is the smallest network needed that can stand on its own, and if you can build one, you can build many more by repeating the same playbook.
evidence: Andrew Chen's research shows Slack's atomic network was under 10 people on a team, while credit cards needed an entire city; the concept scales from there.

text: A product's first atomic network is probably smaller and more specific than expected, often on the order of hundreds of people at a specific moment in time rather than a city or segment.
evidence: Uber's earliest focus was '5pm at the Caltrain station at 5th and King St' rather than all of San Francisco.

text: The next big thing will start out looking like it is for a niche network, which is why new networked products are easy to underestimate.
evidence: Andrew Chen extends Clayton Christensen's Disruption Theory: products look like toys AND target weird niches, making market-size objections misleading.

text: The five most common pitfalls for new PMs are: being a Coordinator (no point of view), a Dictator (micro-managing), a Dreamer (too much strategy), a Feature Factory (shipping without strategy), and a Busted Umbrella (failing to shield the team).
evidence: Lenny's observations across his PM career and from working with many PMs, mapped to five distinct failure modes.

text: Shipping is not success; success is shipping work that has meaningful business impact, high ROI relative to alternatives, and moves you closer to your strategy.
evidence: The Feature Factory pitfall: teams without strategy ship constantly but go nowhere because they lack a filter for what matters.

text: Web3 PMs are responsible for the success of communities rather than increasing acquisition, engagement, or revenue metrics; the role prioritizes execution over vision.
evidence: Jason Shah at Alchemy describes how web3 PM emphasizes building, marketing, and iterating rather than planning, managing, and measuring, with 95/5 execution/vision split.

text: Product managers are not yet common or well understood in web3 because the 0-1 phase depends more on technical infrastructure, cryptoeconomics, and community than on traditional PM skills.
evidence: Nearly half of 20+ web3 teams Jason Shah explored roles with in 2021 did not understand what PMs were for; the Ethereum Foundation has 97 employees with virtually zero PMs.

text: In web3, open-source code and portable user data mean companies cannot rely on proprietary code, trade secrets, or data moats as competitive advantages, forcing continuous product improvement.
evidence: Sushiswap forked Uniswap and conducted a vampire attack; any competitor can fork your product and compete instantly.

text: A technical background is a superpower for product managers because it enables better decisions, understanding of trade-offs, more accurate estimates, and more confident communication with engineers.
evidence: Lenny and Colin Matthews explain that with AI increasingly infused into products, it is even more important that PMs understand technical basics.

text: Almost every software product is built with the same three fundamental parts: a client, a server, and a database, and understanding this architecture helps PMs collaborate more effectively.
evidence: Colin Matthews' breakdown of application architecture shows how these three components interact through APIs in products like Airbnb and OpenAI.

text: Testing is a high-leverage area for PMs to quickly improve product quality; a quick round of end-to-end testing with engineering before a major release catches issues before production.
evidence: Colin Matthews explains that engineers often do not fully understand the whole codebase, making PM-led E2E testing especially valuable.

text: Instead of trying to make something a lot of people like, create something that 100 people love, because once you have that foothold, growth follows through word of mouth and better guests.
evidence: Lenny applied this advice to launch a niche PM podcast that became a top 10 global technology podcast.

text: YouTube is the single best channel for podcast discovery because over half of new listeners come from YouTube's algorithms, a growth channel unmatched by any other platform.
evidence: Lenny's data showing over half of podcast views come from YouTube sending new people to his content.

text: Aim for B+ quality when starting a podcast and work up from there, because putting too much pressure on perfection prevents you from starting at all.
evidence: Lenny shows early episodes of Lex Fridman, Huberman Lab, and other now-popular podcasts that were clearly not A+ quality on day one.

text: A PM's job is to deliver business impact by marshaling the resources of their team to identify and solve the most impactful customer problems.
evidence: Lenny's synthesis from analyzing dozens of PM career ladders and surveying ~1,000 PMs across hundreds of companies.

text: The PM role varies dramatically across companies along two axes: which skills matter most and how much influence a PM has, with Zynga at the mini-CEO end and Apple at the project-manager end.
evidence: Survey data and career ladder analysis across companies like Google, Meta, Stripe, Netflix, and Airbnb showing wide variation.

text: Founding teams should create the first growth model themselves rather than outsourcing it to a Head of Growth hire, because only they understand the DNA of the product and market deeply enough.
evidence: Elena Verna argues that a new Head of Growth will likely copy-paste from past experience rather than build an authentic model aligned with product-market fit.

text: A growth model consists of growth levers (acquisition, retention, monetization) and growth motions (product-led, sales-led, marketing-led), and the unique combination creates a defensible growth strategy.
evidence: Elena Verna shows how SurveyMonkey and Qualtrics competed with nearly the same product but won different segments by applying different growth motions.

text: The first growth hire should be a Builder generalist who proves the growth model's validity, not an Innovator who thinks big or an Optimizer who squeezes marginal gains.
evidence: Elena Verna's framework of three growth profiles (Innovators, Builders, Optimizers) with sequencing advice based on whether the initial growth model hypothesis proves correct.

text: PMs should learn their company's tech stack, sensitive points in the codebase, the build and deploy process, how to contribute small code changes, and technical basics.
evidence: Justin Gage's five areas map to practical PM effectiveness: understanding trade-offs, avoiding delays, and building engineer trust.

text: Being able to make small frontend changes like copy and color fixes yourself will result in more polished products shipped faster and win you points with engineers.
evidence: Justin Gage suggests PMs learn to open PRs for small self-contained changes rather than relying on engineers for typo fixes.

text: Great PMs take pride in the clarity and conciseness of their documents, emails, presentations, and meetings because people judge the quality of thinking by the quality of communication.
evidence: Lenny's observation over years of watching the best PMs, noting that effective communication is the most fundamental PM skill.

text: Great PMs build an aura of 'I have got this' by rarely dropping balls, coming prepared, and ensuring colleagues know that assigned tasks will get done.
evidence: Practical suggestions include being selective with commitments, maintaining a Waiting For list, and communicating priority of new requests.

text: A wedge is a strategy to win a large market by initially capturing a tiny part of a larger market or a large part of a small adjacent market, then expanding from that foothold.
evidence: Twilio started with SMS APIs and became a $60B communication platform; Airbnb started with room rentals and captured 20% of the $90B vacation rental market; Amazon started with books.

text: A wedge is most essential when going after a market that is entrenched or crowded; companies like Zoom, Slack, and Notion won large markets straight-on without a wedge by having an amazing product.
evidence: Datadog CEO Olivier Pomel confirms that some product categories like infrastructure management require broad initial capability and do not work with a wedge.

text: Poor distribution, not product, is the number one cause of startup failure, and getting your product in front of the right people efficiently is increasingly what separates winners from losers.
evidence: Peter Thiel quote supported by data showing free channels like SEO are getting crowded, CPMs are rising, and privacy features are making paid acquisition harder.

text: There are seven early-stage distribution advantages: pre-existing audience, unique viral loop, being first on an emerging platform, remarkable story, pre-existing relationships, strategic partnerships, and extraordinary hustle.
evidence: Examples include Kylie Cosmetics (audience), Tesla (Elon's audience), DoorDash (hustle in Tier 2/3 markets), and Clubhouse (first on audio social).

text: A strong 'why now' is helpful but not essential for building a large business; companies like SpaceX, Airbnb, Pinterest, DoorDash, and Facebook had little or no why-now at founding.
evidence: Research with investors including Sarah Tavel, Ann Miura-Ko, and Garry Tan shows why-now creates advantages but is not mandatory.

text: A strong why-now creates one of two advantages: it unlocks the ability to provide a 10x better product (like smartphones enabling Uber) or it creates a new untapped market need (like remote work enabling Zoom).
evidence: Mapped dozens of companies to their why-now sources including technology shifts (Figma/WebGL), regulatory changes (Eaze/marijuana legalization), and social changes (remote work).

text: There are eight fundamental business model types: sell a thing, rent a thing, take a cut, charge a subscription, charge for usage, sell access, sell attention, and charge per transaction.
evidence: Lenny's original taxonomy mapping all known business models to eight categories with examples like Stripe (take a cut), Netflix (subscription), AWS (usage).

text: The most important metrics to track vary significantly by consumer business type: trial-based subscriptions, freemium subscriptions, ad-based, marketplaces, and DTC each require different metric sets.
evidence: Framework developed with Sriram Krishnan and Yuriy Timen mapping specific metrics to five consumer business types.

text: Greater focus on fewer metrics leads to greater impact; teams should narrow to the two or three metrics that most directly drive business success.
evidence: Lenny's repeated experience across multiple companies finding that excessive metric tracking dilutes effort.

text: Sabbaticals are a peak life experience ranked alongside having a baby or getting married, and not a single person out of 250+ interviewed has regretted taking extended time off.
evidence: DJ DiDonna's research at The Sabbatical Project surveying 250+ people from all walks of life, including those who got divorced or cared for dying loved ones during their sabbatical.

text: The two most important principles for an effective sabbatical are taking enough time off and actually disconnecting from work.
evidence: DJ DiDonna's research showing that partial disconnection undermines the transformational benefits of extended leave.

text: The three biggest myths about corporate sabbatical programs are that everyone will leave, the company will fall apart, and that people do not really need them.
evidence: DJ DiDonna's research across law, consulting, fintech, and nonprofit organizations showing all three fears are unfounded.

text: Nearly one in seven US companies now provide extended leave to employees, and sabbatical programs have become prevalent in the employee benefits arms race for top talent.
evidence: DJ DiDonna's research data on corporate sabbatical adoption trends.

text: The most important factor in early-stage hiring success is the effectiveness of the company's mission or vision in compelling top candidates.
evidence: Founders from Snackpass, Runway, Vori, and others all independently cite captivating vision as the primary reason they attract great talent.

text: Hiring compounds: great early hires create a snowball effect where the quality of the existing team organically attracts more exceptional people.
evidence: Multiple founders confirm the compounding effect, including Siqi Chen at Runway noting that raising the bar becomes easier over time.

text: An IC product manager's performance can be evaluated with three questions in priority order: is the team delivering business impact, are you personally contributing, and do stakeholders regard you highly.
evidence: Lenny's synthesis from his 16-year career, applied as a practical day-to-day self-evaluation tool.

text: The most popular channel for early supply growth in labor marketplaces is posting generic roles to existing job boards like Indeed, ZipRecruiter, and Craigslist.
evidence: Research across a dozen fast-growing labor marketplaces including Clipboard Health, Thumbtack, and Staffy confirms job boards as the dominant early supply channel.

text: The approach to transitioning into a growth PM role is simple: ask your manager for it, learn growth fundamentals through structured study, and then do actual growth work to demonstrate capability.
evidence: Lenny's own transition from generalist PM to growth role at Airbnb by telling his manager, studying, and demonstrating results over six months.

text: Marketplaces are roughly 2x as hard as non-marketplace businesses because you must find product-market fit for both supply and demand.
evidence: Author's direct assertion based on experience evaluating 30+ marketplaces

text: The five most common reasons marketplaces fail are lack of demand-side PMF, lack of supply-side PMF, lack of liquidity, bad unit economics, and scaling too fast.
evidence: Categorization from analysis of 30+ failed marketplace examples including Homejoy, Shyp, Fab, and others

text: Marketplaces beat non-marketplace alternatives on demand by delivering a much cheaper product, a much better product via exclusive supply, or a much better experience via aggregating disaggregated supply.
evidence: Three demand-side advantages mapped to examples: DoorDash (cheaper), Airbnb (better product), GOAT/Uber (better experience)

text: There is generally one fatal issue that kills a marketplace rather than a combination of equal factors.
evidence: Pattern observed across dozens of failed marketplace case studies

text: Take rate is determined by the formula: Convenience + Demand - Competition, where platforms generally charge 5-15% and marketplaces charge 10-50%.
evidence: Analysis of 35+ company take rates showing clear distinction between platforms and marketplaces

text: In winner-take-all markets, charging a lower take rate often beats a higher one because higher fees create friction and opportunity for competitors.
evidence: Bill Gurley's argument in 'A Rake Too Far' plus Jeff Bezos quote 'your margin is my opportunity'

text: OnlyFans can charge a 20% take rate despite not driving demand because it solves a major pain point (accepting payment for sex work) with very low competition.
evidence: Anomaly analysis from take rate dataset showing it charges marketplace rates as a platform

text: In business communication you should start with the answer first and then provide supporting arguments, the opposite of how you naturally arrive at conclusions.
evidence: Barbara Minto's Pyramid Principle developed at McKinsey: 'you think from the bottom up, but you present from the top-down'

text: The SCR (Situation-Complication-Resolution) framework combined with the Minto Pyramid is an effective structure for any business proposal, email, strategy doc, or presentation.
evidence: Applied examples showing before/after of a bug-fix proposal, plus templates for product 1-pagers and strategy docs

text: In a business context you should spend minimal time on Situation and Complication to get to the Resolution as quickly as possible, since colleagues want to be informed not entertained.
evidence: Contrast with storytelling in movies where tension builds; business audiences are busy and want efficiency

text: PMs should trust the designer's expertise on user experience decisions, defer to them in their area of expertise, and protect uninterrupted maker time for creative work.
evidence: Katie Dill's experience leading design at Airbnb, Lyft, and Stripe; Paul Graham's maker vs manager schedule concept

text: Including designers early in the product development process saves time and money because they inform with user voice, catalyze with creative ideas, and gain context for better execution.
evidence: Katie Dill's experience seeing PMs change their tune after including designers in planning

text: A north star vision is essential for product teams to avoid building a disjointed Frankenstein product, and the investment can range from a single-screen whiteboard session to a months-long high-fidelity prototype.
evidence: Lyft's 6-month north star for reimagining the rider app covering multiple transportation modes

text: So-called 'design goals' like consistency, usability, and accessibility are actually business goals because they directly drive conversion, loyalty, and efficiency.
evidence: Katie Dill argues these are sub-goals of business metrics; users notice quality gaps and infer broader failures

text: To protect long-term big-bet projects at large companies, spend 80% of team time on short-term incremental wins and 20% on high-risk long-term bets to create 'cover fire' of consistent results.
evidence: Author's repeated personal experience at Airbnb; supported by advice from PMs at Twitter, Snap, GitHub, Shopify, and Gusto

text: Consistently shipping small wins builds trust, earns the right to take bigger bets, and prevents your team from becoming a target when new fires arise and leaders look for non-essential projects to cut.
evidence: Author's experience plus advice from Sriram Krishnan (keep it small until ready) and Tomer London (small hungry entrepreneurial team)

text: There are five distinct content-driven growth strategies mapped on a 2x2 of SEO vs virality and user-generated vs editorially-generated content: UGSO, EGSO, DGSO, UGVO, and EGVO.
evidence: Author's taxonomy with examples: Quora/Reddit (UGSO), HubSpot/Ahrefs (EGSO), Thumbtack/Zapier (DGSO), TikTok (UGVO), Spotify Wrapped (EGVO)

text: Content marketing does not generate returns as quickly as other forms of marketing, but once you find a format that works you should double down on it rather than trying to do everything at once.
evidence: Intercom's John Collins: 'content is a supertanker, not a speedboat'; Slidebean took 18 months of trial and error on YouTube

text: SEO content is much easier to pursue than viral content because keyword research provides a straightforward source of topic ideas, while viral content depends on creativity.
evidence: Tim Soulo of Ahrefs contrasting the two approaches based on years of content production

text: In HubSpot's early days, over 30% of customers came from demand generated by blog posts, and they think of competitors as other media outlets rather than other SaaS brands.
evidence: HubSpot's head of content Kieran Flanagan sharing internal data

text: The three most important differentiators of senior PMs versus junior PMs are strategy, autonomy, and nuance rather than better day-to-day execution.
evidence: Jackie Bavaro's research for Cracking the PM Career; most of the senior PM's strategic work is invisible to teammates

text: A product strategy has three components -- vision, strategic framework, and roadmap -- and newer PMs prioritize by incremental value while senior PMs prioritize by progress toward long-term vision.
evidence: Jackie Bavaro's framework with toy-game examples illustrating each component

text: The median time to progress from entry-level PM to senior PM is about 3 years, making it the longest single milestone in the PM career path.
evidence: Lenny's estimate based on experience: 3 years to Senior, 1-2 to GPM, 2-3 to Director

text: Most companies find the vast majority of growth from just one of four engines -- virality, performance marketing, content, or sales -- and optimizing a secondary engine before scaling the core one is rarely time well spent.
evidence: Author's research for First Round and Reforge on growth engines

text: There are seven distinct types of virality across three categories: word-of-mouth (offline and online), invitation (social, incentivized, utility), and experiential (passive and active).
evidence: Taxonomy built from dozens of examples including Airbnb, Clubhouse, Dropbox, TikTok, Calendly, and Citizen
